mnemonics.title: Pumping Lemma CFL Structure
mnemonics.content: For CFL, any long string 's' can be broken into u-v-x-y-z. V and Y get pumped (repeated) while x stays the middle anchor. The rule: vxy ≤ p and vy ≥ 1. Then uv^i x y^i z stays in L for all i ≥ 0.

mnemonics.title: The Five Brothers
mnemonics.content: Think of u, v, x, y, z as five friends. Two of them (v and y) are the 'pumping brothers' - they can repeat themselves any number of times while x remains the steady center. The rule: don't pump nothing (vy ≥ 1) and stay close (vxy ≤ p).

mnemonics.title: Not a Toy Language
mnemonics.content: Use Pumping Lemma to prove language is NOT CFL by showing for ALL decompositions, some i makes uv^i x y^i z escape the language. If you find ONE string that fails, the language isn't context-free!

acronyms.expansion: V and Y are Important Parts (the pumping segments)
acronyms.usage: Remember: v and y are the parts we pump up (repeat). Without them, we can't prove non-CFL.

acronyms.expansion: 3 conditions: |vxy|≤p, |vy|≥1, uv^i xy^i z ∈ L. 2 pumping parts (v,y). 1 middle section (x)
acronyms.usage: When applying the lemma, check all 3 conditions hold for the decomposition.

acronyms.expansion: Not In the Pumpable set - prove language is NOT CFL by showing pumping fails
acronyms.usage: If you find a string where NO decomposition satisfies the lemma, language is NOT context-free.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a string as a sandwich: u [v] x [y] z. The brackets [] are v and y - the parts that get duplicated. The middle x is the 'meat' that stays fixed. The whole thing: u-v-x-y-z with v and y being the pumpable middle layers.
Draw the string as a horizontal bar. Mark positions for u, v, x, y, z. Show arrows indicating v and y expanding/contracting while x remains stationary. Use i=0,1,2 to show repetitions.

Draw a vertical line at position p on your string. Everything after p can be decomposed. The vxy portion must fit entirely within the first p characters - draw a box showing vxy within the first p positions.
Two columns: Left shows 'Assume CFL', Right shows 'Find counter-example'. Arrow from left to right labeled 'Contradiction' when you find string that fails.

analogies.analogy: Think of CFL like a song with a repeating chorus (v and y) and verses (u, x, z). The pumping lemma says if the song is long enough, you can repeat the chorus any number of times and it remains a valid song in the language.
v and y are like springs attached to x. You can stretch them (i>1) or compress them (i=0) but x stays fixed. As long as the springs don't disappear completely (|vy|≥1), the structure holds.
Think of the string as lungs breathing. Inhale (i=0) - v and y contract. Exhale (i=2+) - v and y expand. Normal breath (i=1) is the original. The x is the trachea that stays constant.

In CFL, derivations are trees. The pumping lemma is like pruning and regrowing branches. v and y are subtrees you can duplicate. As long as you keep at least one branch (|vy|≥1) and don't grow beyond bounds (|vxy|≤p), the tree remains valid.
